raw_case:
Heterotopic pancreas — An expert in hide and seek


Introduction and importance

Heterotopic pancreas is rare and difficult to diagnose pre-operatively. Common sites for heterotopic pancreas such as the upper gastrointestinal tract, rarely involve the thorax, spleen, and kidney. Endoscopy imaging and CT imaging have been advocated to aid in diagnosis; however, they still present challenges. The majority of heterotopic pancreas patients are asymptomatic.

Case presentation

Our patient presented left-sided abdominal pain intermittently, each time after meals, with weight loss over the past three months. Imaging showed a small intraluminal growth at the posterior wall of the duodenum, with no metabolic activity detected from the PET scan. Given the symptomatic presentation, we proceeded with laparotomy and small bowel resection with primary anastomosis. HPE confirmed the presence of a heterotopic pancreas. Here, we discuss the management of the heterotopic pancreas and provide a literature review.

Clinical discussion

The heterotopic pancreas shares a similar genetic makeup, physiological function, and local environment exposure as the pancreas. Similar complications, such as acute or chronic pancreatitis, abscesses, pseudocyst formation, and, rarely malignancy can also occur in the heterotopic pancreas. It is often difficult to detect a heterotopic pancreas. Factors such as the site, size, and characteristics of the lesion usually aid in diagnosis, whether through endoscopy or imaging. Biopsies can be taken via endoscopy if the lesion is accessible, but most biopsies are non-diagnostic.

Conclusion

Heterotopic pancreas is rare and challenging to diagnose. Knowledge of common locations and characteristic imaging appearances of heterotopic pancreatic tissue aids in diagnosing. The definitive treatment is surgical resection.

Highlights

•Heterotopic pancreas is extremely rare and difficult to diagnose pre-operatively.•Majority of the patients often present asymptomatic.•Gold standard diagnosis is histopathological examination.1

Introduction and information

Heterotopic pancreas is a rare congenital anomaly in which pancreatic tissue is ectopically present outside the main pancreatic gland, without vascular or ductal continuity. Common sites include the gastrointestinal tract such as the stomach, duodenum, and jejunum. It has also been reported in rarer locations such as the thorax, kidney, spleen, and biliary tract.Patients are often asymptomatic majority of cases. It is typically diagnosed incidentally during unrelated surgery, imaging or at autopsy [1]. Wei R etc. reported symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and weight loss in some patients [2]. Diagnosing heterotopic pancreas remains challenging, even with technological advancement, due to the low incidence rate of 0.11–0.21 % at autopsy, with a male to female ratio of 3:1 [3]. This report aims to elucidate the presentation and management of this rare diagnosis.2

Case presentation

A 45-year-old gentleman, with underlying diabetes mellitus and hypertension presented to the tertiary centre with intermitted left-sided abdominal pain for three months, occasionally accompanied by vomiting 2 h postprandial and weight loss. Upon examination, the abdomen was non-tender with no palpable mass. The initial impression was intussusception in adults, with differentials including duodenal GIST, bilary colic or duodenal malignancy; imaging was subsequently performed.Ultrasound of the abdomen revealed gallbladder polyps with cholelithiasis and fatty liver, but no obvious mass was seen. A Computer tomography (CT) scan (Fig. 1) showed a small soft tissue lesion at the posterior wall of the fourth part of the duodenum. Oesophagoduodeoscopy and colonoscopy findings were normal. Push enteroscopy was performed up to the proximal jejunum, showing no luminal lesions, narrowing or stricture. PET CT (Fig. 1b) indicated a persistent duodenal lesion that was not metabolically active. A CT of the abdomen/pelvis in prone position (Fig. 1c) revealed a persistent intramural tumour at the posterior wall of D4.Blood parameters and tumour markers were within the normal range. The patient underwent open cholecystectomy with D3/D4 small bowel resection and side-to-side duodenojejunostomy anastomosis. Intraoperatively, a 1 cm lesion was palpable over the D3/D4 junction (Fig. 2), with no liver lesions or suspicious peritoneal nodules. HPE of the gallbladder showed features of chronic cholecystitis with no malignancy. HPE of the duodenum specimen (Fig. 3) revealed a whitish nodule composed of lobules of benign pancreatic acini with intervening benign ductal elements. No islets of Langerhans were detected, and no malignancy was noted, suggesting the presence of a heterotopic pancreas. The patient was discharged well after seven days postoperatively, with no immediate complications. During his routine follow up in the clinic, his symptoms had resolved and he was doing well.This work has been reported in line with the SCARE criteria [10].3

Clinical discussion

The heterotopic pancreas shares similar genetic makeup, physiologic function and local environment exposure to the pancreas. Most agreeable theory suggested that during the development of pancreas from several invaginations which originate from the wall of primitive duodenum, one or more evaginations may remain in bowel wall, giving rise to ectopic pancreas in the duodenum, as observed in our case. In 1909, Heinrich described subtypes of heterotopic pancreas based on the components of normal pancreatic tissue, including acini, ducts and islet cells.●Type I is characterized by the presence of typical pancreatic tissues with acini, ducts, and islet cells similar to those seen in a normal pancreas.●Type II features pancreatic ducts and acini, but lacks islet cells, aligning with our case.●Type III consists of ducts with few acini or only dilated ducts, referred to as adenomyoma.●Type IV is characterized solely by the presence of islet cells [4].Similar complications in the pancreas such as acute or chronic pancreatitis, abscesses and pseudocyst formation, and, rarely, malignancy can also manifest in heterotopic pancreas [5]. Our patient experienced intermittent left-sided abdominal pain after meals, which may be attributed to the secretion of exocrine and endocrine hormones by the heterotopic pancreas, leading to inflammation or chemical irritation of the surrounding tissue. Armstrong CP et al. also reported haemorrhage due to mucosal erosion or ulcer formation in small intestine, secondary to hormone production by heterotopic pancreas [6].Detecting heterotopic pancreas can be challenging. The site, size, and lesion often aid diagnosis, whether through endoscopy or imaging. Biopsies can be performed via endoscopy if the lesion is accessible, but most biopsies tend to be non-diagnostic. Endoscopic ultrasonography with fine needle aspiration can help identify pancreatic rest located in the submucosa with a depth of less than 2 cm, allowing cytological evaluation of the lesion. A retrospective study by Ran Wei et al., described heterotopic pancreas on endoscopic imaging as an endoluminal, ill-defined, submucosal mass with typically central umbilication. On CT imaging, heterotopic pancreas may appear as submucosal masses, ill-defined lesions with an endoluminal growth pattern, bright enhancement similar to normal pancreatic tissue, surface dimpling and low intralesional attenuation [7,8]. Our patient underwent a subsequent CT scan in the prone position, initially planned to dislodge any ‘foreign body’ deposited at the posterior wall of the duodenum; the repeated scan showed the same lesion persisted.According to LeCompte et al., they symptoms of heterotopic pancreas depend on the anatomical and histological location and functionality of the lesion [9]. Our patient's HP is located in the submucosa, which can cause local chemical irritation to the surrounding tissue, resulting in pain and intermittent obstructive symptoms.Heterotopic pancreas is often difficult to diagnose with imaging and endoscopy. CT imaging typically reports an ill-defined submucosal lesion, while endoscopy shows a submucosal mass with central umbilication. Our patient underwent surgery due to pain with weight loss, initially suspected to have a GIST of the duodenum, which ultimately turned out to be a heterotopic pancreas. Expectant management for complications of pancreas should be similar for the heterotopic pancreas; however, surgical intervention can provide relief for symptomatic patients.4

Conclusion

Heterotopic pancreas is rare and challenging to diagnose. Knowledge of common locations and characteristic imaging appearances of heterotopic pancreatic tissue will aid in the diagnosis. The definitive treatment is surgical resection.

Abbreviations


OGDSesophagoduodenoscopyCTcomputerized tomographyHPEhistopathological examinationUSGultrasoundHPheterotropic pancreas


Consent

Written consent was obtained from the patient for the publication of this case report and accompanying images.

Ethical approval

Ethics approval was not required from the institution for the publication of this case report.

Funding

This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Author contribution

KP Tiang responsible for write-up, and design of the write up, Kamarajan, Lee, Yu, Arisha, Koh responsible for write up, data collection, analysis, picture and proof checking.

Guarantor

Ko-Ping Tiang will be the guarantor.

Declaration of competing interest

There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 45-year-old male with diabetes mellitus and hypertension.
- **Chief Complaint:** Intermittent, left-sided abdominal pain after meals for three months with associated weight loss.
- **History of Present Illness:** Symptoms included abdominal pain occurring postprandially, occasional vomiting two hours after meals, and weight loss over a three-month duration.
- **Past Medical History:** Diabetes mellitus and hypertension.
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Abdomen was non-tender; no palpable masses noted.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Ultrasound: Gallbladder polyps, cholelithiasis, fatty liver; no obvious masses.
  - CT scan: Small soft tissue lesion at the posterior wall of the fourth part of the duodenum.
  - Endoscopy (oesophagoduodenoscopy and colonoscopy): Normal findings.
  - Push enteroscopy: No luminal lesions, narrowing, or strictures in the proximal jejunum.
  - PET-CT scan: Persistent duodenal lesion without metabolic activity.
  - Blood parameters and tumor markers: Within normal limits.
  - Surgical Findings: 1 cm lesion at the D3/D4 junction; no liver lesions or peritoneal nodules.
  - Histopathological Examination (HPE): Lobules of benign pancreatic acini and ducts without malignancy, consistent with heterotopic pancreas.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. The initial presentation of postprandial abdominal pain and weight loss prompted consideration of:
   - **Duodenal gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST):** Possible given imaging findings of a submucosal lesion. Excluded as PET-CT showed no metabolic activity and HPE confirmed no malignancy.
   - **Biliary colic:** Gallbladder polyps and cholelithiasis seen on ultrasound could explain abdominal pain but not weight loss or duodenal lesion. Excluded after histopathology revealed findings consistent with chronic cholecystitis only.
   - **Duodenal malignancy:** Suggested by symptoms and imaging of a duodenal lesion. Excluded as tumor markers were normal, imaging lacked concerning features, and HPE did not reveal malignancy.
2. Persistent imaging findings of a submucosal lesion led to surgical resection for definitive diagnosis. HPE identified heterotopic pancreatic tissue (Type II classification: pancreatic ducts and acini without islet cells).
3. Other rare possibilities (e.g., intussusception due to polypoid lesions) were excluded based on lack of imaging and intraoperative findings.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Heterotopic pancreas located in the submucosa of the duodenum, presenting with postprandial pain and weight loss. Diagnosis was confirmed through surgical resection and histopathological examination.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> Analyze the chief complaint and history of present illness. The patient presents with intermittent, left-sided abdominal pain after meals for three months, associated with vomiting two hours postprandially and weight loss. This symptom pattern suggests a potential issue in the upper gastrointestinal tract that is exacerbated by food intake. The postprandial nature of the pain points towards a problem in the stomach, duodenum, or proximal jejunum. Weight loss indicates chronicity and potentially a more serious underlying condition.

<step 2> Review the past medical history. The patient has diabetes mellitus and hypertension. These are chronic conditions but not directly explaining the current abdominal symptoms, although diabetes can sometimes be associated with gastroparesis, which can cause postprandial symptoms, but the vomiting 2 hours after meal and location description might not fit gastroparesis perfectly.

<step 3> Evaluate the physical examination findings. The abdomen was non-tender and no masses were palpated. This is somewhat reassuring but does not rule out intra-abdominal pathology, especially smaller lesions or those not readily palpable.

<step 4> Examine the ancillary test results systematically.
    - **Ultrasound:** Gallbladder polyps, cholelithiasis, and fatty liver were found. While gallbladder issues can cause abdominal pain, the pain is typically right-sided and often biliary colic is more acute.  Fatty liver is likely related to diabetes and less likely to be the primary cause of the current symptoms.
    - **CT scan:** A small soft tissue lesion at the posterior wall of the fourth part of the duodenum was identified. This is a crucial finding and a potential source of the patient's symptoms. The location in the duodenum is consistent with postprandial pain and vomiting.
    - **Endoscopy (EGD and Colonoscopy):** Normal findings in both.  While EGD visualizes the duodenum, smaller or submucosal lesions, or lesions in the deeper parts of the duodenum (D3/D4) might be missed, especially if they are not causing luminal changes. Colonoscopy is less relevant for upper abdominal pain.
    - **Push enteroscopy:** Normal findings in the proximal jejunum. This extends the endoscopic examination further down, but again, a lesion in the D4 could be at the limit or just beyond the typical reach and might be missed if it's not causing luminal narrowing.
    - **PET-CT scan:** Persistent duodenal lesion without metabolic activity. The lack of metabolic activity on PET-CT strongly suggests a benign nature of the lesion, making malignancy less probable.
    - **Blood parameters and tumor markers:** Within normal limits. This further reduces the likelihood of aggressive malignancy.
    - **Surgical Findings:** Confirmation of a 1 cm lesion at the D3/D4 junction. This confirms the CT finding and allows for direct visualization and biopsy. The absence of liver lesions or peritoneal nodules during surgery is also a good sign, suggesting no metastasis.
    - **Histopathological Examination (HPE):**  The HPE report is definitive. It shows lobules of benign pancreatic acini and ducts without malignancy, consistent with heterotopic pancreas.

<step 5> Integrate all findings to reach a final diagnosis. The HPE result of heterotopic pancreas is the key finding. Heterotopic pancreas in the duodenum is a known entity and can cause abdominal pain, sometimes postprandial, and can even lead to obstructive symptoms in some cases. Given the location of the lesion in D3/D4, the postprandial pain, and the definitive HPE result, the heterotopic pancreas is the most likely cause of the patient's symptoms. While gallbladder issues were noted on ultrasound, they are less likely to be the primary cause given the location of the lesion in the duodenum and the HPE result.

<step 6> Conclude on the final diagnosis based on the comprehensive analysis. The most appropriate final diagnosis is symptomatic heterotopic pancreas of the duodenum, causing postprandial abdominal pain and weight loss.

### Answer:
Heterotopic pancreas of the duodenum

reasoning_eval.step: Analyze the chief complaint and history of present illness. The patient presents with intermittent, left-sided abdominal pain after meals for three months, associated with vomiting two hours postprandially and weight loss. This symptom pattern suggests a potential issue in the upper gastrointestinal tract that is exacerbated by food intake. The postprandial nature of the pain points towards a problem in the stomach, duodenum, or proximal jejunum. Weight loss indicates chronicity and potentially a more serious underlying condition.

Review the past medical history. The patient has diabetes mellitus and hypertension. These are chronic conditions but not directly explaining the current abdominal symptoms, although diabetes can sometimes be associated with gastroparesis, which can cause postprandial symptoms, but the vomiting 2 hours after meal and location description might not fit gastroparesis perfectly.

Evaluate the physical examination findings. The abdomen was non-tender and no masses were palpated. This is somewhat reassuring but does not rule out intra-abdominal pathology, especially smaller lesions or those not readily palpable.

Examine the ancillary test results systematically.
    - **Ultrasound:** Gallbladder polyps, cholelithiasis, and fatty liver were found. While gallbladder issues can cause abdominal pain, the pain is typically right-sided and often biliary colic is more acute.  Fatty liver is likely related to diabetes and less likely to be the primary cause of the current symptoms.
    - **CT scan:** A small soft tissue lesion at the posterior wall of the fourth part of the duodenum was identified. This is a crucial finding and a potential source of the patient's symptoms. The location in the duodenum is consistent with postprandial pain and vomiting.
    - **Endoscopy (EGD and Colonoscopy):** Normal findings in both.  While EGD visualizes the duodenum, smaller or submucosal lesions, or lesions in the deeper parts of the duodenum (D3/D4) might be missed, especially if they are not causing luminal changes. Colonoscopy is less relevant for upper abdominal pain.
    - **Push enteroscopy:** Normal findings in the proximal jejunum. This extends the endoscopic examination further down, but again, a lesion in the D4 could be at the limit or just beyond the typical reach and might be missed if it's not causing luminal narrowing.
    - **PET-CT scan:** Persistent duodenal lesion without metabolic activity. The lack of metabolic activity on PET-CT strongly suggests a benign nature of the lesion, making malignancy less probable.
    - **Blood parameters and tumor markers:** Within normal limits. This further reduces the likelihood of aggressive malignancy.
    - **Surgical Findings:** Confirmation of a 1 cm lesion at the D3/D4 junction. This confirms the CT finding and allows for direct visualization and biopsy. The absence of liver lesions or peritoneal nodules during surgery is also a good sign, suggesting no metastasis.
    - **Histopathological Examination (HPE):**  The HPE report is definitive. It shows lobules of benign pancreatic acini and ducts without malignancy, consistent with heterotopic pancreas.

Integrate all findings to reach a final diagnosis. The HPE result of heterotopic pancreas is the key finding. Heterotopic pancreas in the duodenum is a known entity and can cause abdominal pain, sometimes postprandial, and can even lead to obstructive symptoms in some cases. Given the location of the lesion in D3/D4, the postprandial pain, and the definitive HPE result, the heterotopic pancreas is the most likely cause of the patient's symptoms. While gallbladder issues were noted on ultrasound, they are less likely to be the primary cause given the location of the lesion in the duodenum and the HPE result.

Conclude on the final diagnosis based on the comprehensive analysis. The most appropriate final diagnosis is symptomatic heterotopic pancreas of the duodenum, causing postprandial abdominal pain and weight loss.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial presentation of postprandial abdominal pain and weight loss suggested possible conditions including duodenal GIST, biliary colic, and duodenal malignancy. Excluded GIST due to no metabolic activity on PET-CT and confirmed benign nature via HPE. Biliary colic excluded as ultrasound showed gallbladder polyps and cholelithiasis with findings consistent only of chronic cholecystitis. Duodenal malignancy excluded with normal tumor markers, lack of concerning imaging features, and benign HPE results.

Persistent imaging findings of a submucosal lesion led to surgical resection for definitive diagnosis. Histopathology identified heterotopic pancreatic tissue (Type II classification: pancreatic ducts and acini without islet cells).
Other rare possibilities such as intussusception due to polypoid lesions were excluded based on lack of supporting imaging and intraoperative findings.

Final diagnosis: Heterotopic pancreas located in the submucosa of the duodenum, presenting with postprandial pain and weight loss. Confirmed through surgical resection and histopathological examination.
